perturbation.original_text: 9. In consideration of the Confidential Information being furnished hereby, each party agrees that, without the prior written consent of the Chief Financial Officer or General Counsel of the other party, for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement neither it nor any of its affiliates will, either directly or indirectly, solicit for employment, or otherwise contract for the services of (or cause or seek to cause to leave the employ of other party or any of its affiliates) any person who is now employed or engaged (either as an employee or consultant) or becomes employed or engaged during the term of this Agreement by the other party or its affiliates, other than persons whose employment or engagement shall have been terminated at least six (6) months prior to the date of such solicitation, employment or other contractual arrangements. The prohibition contained in this paragraph does not extend to general solicitations of employment by a party not specifically directed towards the other party’s employees or consultants including but not limited to (i) advertising on the internet or in a newspaper or periodical of general circulation, (ii) an employee of an executive search firm acting on behalf of the hiring company where the hiring company did not instruct or encourage such solicitation, (iii) a general hiring program conducted by the hiring party in the ordinary course of business (such as a job fair or the like), or (iv) a response by the hiring party if the employee approaches the hiring party on an unsolicited basis.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. In consideration of the Confidential Information being furnished hereby, each party agrees that, without the prior written consent of the Chief Financial Officer or General Counsel of the other party, for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement neither it nor any of its affiliates will, either directly or indirectly, solicit for employment, or otherwise contract for the services of (or cause or seek to cause to leave the employ of other party or any of its affiliates) any person who is now employed or engaged (either as an employee or consultant) or becomes employed or engaged during the term of this Agreement by the other party or its affiliates. The prohibition contained in this paragraph does not extend to general solicitations of employment by a party not specifically directed towards the other party’s employees or consultants, but it could extend if the parties decided so by mutual agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause provides clear exceptions to the non-solicitation agreement, outlining specific instances where general solicitations are permitted. The modified version adds "but it could extend if the parties decided so by mutual agreement". This conditional statement directly contradicts the initial statement that the prohibition does not extend to general solicitations, creating uncertainty about the scope of the non-solicitation clause. This introduces in-text contradiction, where it states that the parties can decide whether to extend the prohibition, even if it states above that 'the prohibition does not extend to general solicitations of employment'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In consideration of the Confidential Information being furnished hereby, each party agrees that, without the prior written consent of the Chief Financial Officer or General Counsel of the other party, for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement neither it nor any of its affiliates will, either directly or indirectly, solicit for employment, or otherwise contract for the services of (or cause or seek to cause to leave the employ of other party or any of its affiliates) any person who is now employed or engaged (either as an employee or consultant) or becomes employed or engaged during the term of this Agreement by the other party or its affiliates. The prohibition contained in this paragraph does not extend to general solicitations of employment by a party not specifically directed towards the other party’s employees or consultants, but it could extend if the parties decided so by mutual agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction. The original text states that the non-solicitation prohibition *does not* extend to general solicitations, but the changed text adds 'but it could extend if the parties decided so by mutual agreement.' This creates a clear inconsistency, as it implies that the prohibition *can* extend to general solicitations if mutually agreed, directly contradicting the preceding statement. Since the contradicted text is in the same location of the changed text, but it still contradicts itself, thus causing a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1. During the evaluation of the Proposed Transaction, the Recipient agrees to treat confidentially information that is furnished by either the Disclosing Party or its Representatives (as defined below) to the Recipient or the Recipient’s subsidiaries or its or their respective directors, officers, partners, employees, agents, representatives, including, without limitation, financial advisors, counsel, accountants, experts, and consultants (collectively, “Representatives”) in connection with the Proposed Transaction, together with notes, analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by the Recipient or by its Representatives to the extent they contain or otherwise reflect such information or the Disclosing Party’s review of, or interest in, the Recipient (“Confidential Information”).
perturbation.changed_text: 1. During the evaluation of the Proposed Transaction, the Recipient agrees to treat as potentially confidential information that is furnished by either the Disclosing Party or its Representatives (as defined below) to the Recipient or the Recipient’s subsidiaries or its or their respective directors, officers, partners, employees, agents, representatives, including, without limitation, financial advisors, counsel, accountants, experts, and consultants (collectively, “Representatives”) in connection with the Proposed Transaction, together with notes, analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by the Recipient or by its Representatives to the extent they contain or otherwise reflect such information or the Disclosing Party’s review of, or interest in, the Recipient (“Confidential Information”).
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the Recipient agrees to treat furnished information *confidentially*. The changed text alters this to 'agrees to treat as *potentially* confidential information'. This introduces an ambiguity and weakens the obligation, contradicting the very purpose of a Non-Disclosure Agreement. It is not clear whether the Recipient must treat it confidentially, or if it has discretion in determining what is truly confidential. This change introduces in-text contradiction with the title of the document: Mutual Non-Disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: During the evaluation of the Proposed Transaction, the Recipient agrees to treat as potentially confidential information that is furnished by either the Disclosing Party or its Representatives (as defined below) to the Recipient or the Recipient’s subsidiaries or its or their respective directors, officers, partners, employees, agents, representatives, including, without limitation, financial advisors, counsel, accountants, experts, and consultants (collectively, “Representatives”) in connection with the Proposed Transaction, together with notes, analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by the Recipient or by its Representatives to the extent they contain or otherwise reflect such information or the Disclosing Party’s review of, or interest in, the Recipient (“Confidential Information”).
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original text from requiring the Recipient to treat furnished information 'confidentially' to treating it as 'potentially confidential information'. This introduces ambiguity where there was previously a clear obligation. The contradicted text is located in the same section, highlighting the in-text nature of the contradiction. By weakening the obligation of confidentiality, the change introduces an in-text contradiction with the title of the document: Mutual Non-Disclosure Agreement, as it is no longer clear whether the information is actually subject to non-disclosure, which is a structural flaw.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall at all times remain, as between the parties, the property of the Disclosing Party. No license under any trade secrets, copyrights, or other rights is granted by this Agreement or any disclosure of Confidential Information hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall at all times remain, as between the parties, the property of both the Disclosing Party and the Recipient, with the Disclosing Party retaining original ownership. No license under any trade secrets, copyrights, or other rights is granted by this Agreement or any disclosure of Confidential Information hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies that Confidential Information remains the property of the Disclosing Party. The changed text introduces the idea that it becomes the property of both parties, while also trying to retain the Disclosing Party's 'original ownership'. This is contradictory: something cannot simultaneously be the property of two parties, especially if one retains 'original ownership'. This creates confusion as to who has the right to use, disclose, or protect the information. This change introduces in-text contradiction in the ownership of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall at all times remain, as between the parties, the property of both the Disclosing Party and the Recipient, with the Disclosing Party retaining original ownership. No license under any trade secrets, copyrights, or other rights is granted by this Agreement or any disclosure of Confidential Information hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that Confidential Information remains the property of the Disclosing Party. The changed text introduces a direct conflict by stating that it becomes the property of both the Disclosing Party and the Recipient, while simultaneously claiming the Disclosing Party retains 'original ownership.' This is logically inconsistent and creates an in-text contradiction as the same piece of information cannot be wholly owned by two separate parties, especially with one having 'original ownership'. The contradicted text section emphasizes this conflict since the contradicted text is identical to the changed text, indicating that the changed text itself introduces the contradiction and that the property rights are not being maintained in a way that's consistent.

perturbation.original_text: 13. The parties agree that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by a party or its Representatives and that, in addition to all other available remedies, either party may be entitled to seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. The parties agree that monetary damages shall be considered the primary and sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by a party or its Representatives; however, in exceptional circumstances, subject to mutual written agreement, either party may be entitled to seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause acknowledges that monetary damages may *not* be a sufficient remedy and allows for other remedies like specific performance. The modified version directly contradicts this by stating that monetary damages *shall* be considered the primary and sufficient remedy. The addition of 'in exceptional circumstances' and 'subject to mutual written agreement' weakens the ability to seek other remedies, creating a contradiction between the main clause and the added conditions. This change introduces in-text contradiction by stating that monetary damages are enough, but allows parties to seek specific performance when necessary.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties agree that monetary damages shall be considered the primary and sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by a party or its Representatives; however, in exceptional circumstances, subject to mutual written agreement, either party may be entitled to seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction. The original text allows for remedies beyond monetary damages, while the changed text states that monetary damages 'shall be considered the primary and sufficient remedy,' directly conflicting with the original allowance for other remedies. Even with the addition of 'in exceptional circumstances,' the primary assertion contradicts the idea that monetary damages might *not* be sufficient as stated, triggering a structural flaw - in text contradiction as the clause is effectively contradicting itself.
